topic.title: Grouped-Query Attention
topic.question: Why does grouped-query attention reduce inference cost compared to multi-head attention, and what does it trade away?

lesson.title: Grouped-Query Attention: Slashing KV-Cache Cost
lesson.payoff: GQA cuts KV-cache memory by a factor of G (number of query heads divided by number of KV heads), reducing memory bandwidth pressure at inference time with minimal quality loss
staircase: Understand what multi-head attention stores in the KV cache
See why that cache is the inference bottleneck
Introduce MHA notation: H query heads, H key heads, H value heads
Contrast with multi-query attention: H query heads, 1 KV head
Define grouped-query attention: H query heads, G KV head groups where 1 < G < H
Derive the memory reduction factor: KV cache shrinks by H/G
Understand the quality tradeoff: less expressive KV diversity per query
lesson.destination: GQA partitions H query heads into G groups, each group sharing one KV head pair, reducing KV-cache size by factor H/G and memory-bandwidth cost proportionally, at the cost of reduced per-head KV expressiveness compared to full MHA

narration.fullText: Let us start with multi-head attention, abbreviated MHA, the standard mechanism in transformer models where a sequence of tokens attends to itself. In MHA, we have H query heads, H key heads, and H value heads — all equal. At each decoding step during inference, we must store all past keys and values in a structure called the KV cache, so we do not recompute them. That cache size scales as 2 times H times d_k times the sequence length L, where d_k is the per-head dimension. This is not a compute problem — it is a memory bandwidth problem: the GPU must stream this entire cache from high-bandwidth memory on every decoding step, and that streaming is what limits throughput. Now consider the extreme: multi-query attention, or MQA, proposed by Shazeer in 2019, which uses H query heads but only one shared key head and one shared value head. The KV cache shrinks by a factor of H, which is dramatic, but quality degrades noticeably because all queries share identical key and value projections. Grouped-query attention, or GQA, introduced by Ainslie et al. in 2023, splits the difference. We keep H query heads but use G key-value head groups, where G is strictly between 1 and H. Each group of H-over-G query heads shares one KV head pair. The KV cache now holds 2 times G times d_k times L entries instead of 2 times H times d_k times L, giving a reduction factor of H divided by G. For example, Llama 3 8B uses H equals 32 query heads and G equals 8 KV heads, so the KV cache is four times smaller than full MHA. The tradeoff is representational: within each group, every query head applies the same key and value projection, so the model has less capacity to learn diverse attention patterns across heads than full MHA. In practice, models trained with GQA from scratch recover nearly all of the MHA quality while enjoying the bandwidth savings, making GQA the default architecture choice for large-scale inference today.

beats.text: Let us start with multi-head attention, abbreviated MHA, the standard mechanism in transformer models where a sequence of tokens attends to itself.
beats.whyThisBeat: Establish the baseline architecture and introduce MHA abbreviation

beats.text: In MHA, we have H query heads, H key heads, and H value heads — all equal.
beats.whyThisBeat: First rung: state the symmetric head structure of MHA

beats.text: At each decoding step during inference, we must store all past keys and values in a structure called the KV cache, so we do not recompute them.
beats.whyThisBeat: Define the KV cache and explain why it exists

beats.text: That cache size scales as 2 times H times d_k times the sequence length L, where d_k is the per-head dimension. This is not a compute problem — it is a memory bandwidth problem: the GPU must stream this entire cache from high-bandwidth memory on every decoding step.
beats.whyThisBeat: Second rung: quantify the KV cache and name the bottleneck as memory bandwidth, not compute

beats.text: Now consider the extreme: multi-query attention, or MQA, which uses H query heads but only one shared key head and one shared value head. The KV cache shrinks by a factor of H, which is dramatic, but quality degrades noticeably.
beats.whyThisBeat: Third rung: introduce MQA as the extreme reduction and its quality cost, motivating the middle ground

beats.text: Grouped-query attention, or GQA, splits the difference. We keep H query heads but use G key-value head groups, where G is strictly between 1 and H. Each group of H-over-G query heads shares one KV head pair.
beats.whyThisBeat: Fourth rung: define GQA precisely with the grouping structure

beats.text: The KV cache now holds 2 times G times d_k times L entries instead of 2 times H times d_k times L, giving a reduction factor of H divided by G.
beats.whyThisBeat: Fifth rung: derive the memory reduction formula

beats.text: For example, Llama 3 8B uses H equals 32 query heads and G equals 8 KV heads, so the KV cache is four times smaller than full MHA.
beats.whyThisBeat: Anchor the formula with a concrete real-world number

beats.text: The tradeoff is representational: within each group, every query head applies the same key and value projection, so the model has less capacity to learn diverse attention patterns across heads than full MHA.
beats.whyThisBeat: Sixth rung: articulate what GQA trades away in terms of expressive capacity

beats.text: In practice, models trained with GQA from scratch recover nearly all of the MHA quality while enjoying the bandwidth savings, making GQA the default architecture choice for large-scale inference today.
beats.whyThisBeat: Destination: summarize the empirical verdict and practical conclusion

actions.content: Multi-Head Attention (MHA)
H query heads,  H key heads,  H value heads
Draw 4 rectangles in a row labeled Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 below a row of 4 rectangles labeled K1 K2 K3 K4 and another row labeled V1 V2 V3 V4, with vertical arrows connecting each Qi to its matching Ki and Vi
KV Cache: stores K,V for all past tokens
Draw a tall rectangle on the right side labeled KV Cache with horizontal lines suggesting stacked rows, annotated with arrow pointing right labeled 'grows with sequence length L'
Cache size = 2 * H * d_k * L
Bottleneck: memory bandwidth (not FLOPs)
kvcache_formula
Multi-Query Attention (MQA): H query heads, 1 KV head
Draw 4 rectangles in a row labeled Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4, with 4 arrows converging to a single rectangle labeled K and another single rectangle labeled V below it
MQA Cache size = 2 * 1 * d_k * L   (H x smaller)
MQA: quality degrades (single KV projection for all queries)
all
all
all
Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)

Draw 4 rectangles in a row labeled Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4. Draw 2 rectangles below labeled KV-group-1 and KV-group-2. Draw arrows: Q1 and Q2 both point to KV-group-1; Q3 and Q4 both point to KV-group-2. Label the grouping bracket 'G=2 KV groups, H=4 query heads'
G KV groups, where 1 < G < H
GQA Cache size = 2 * G * d_k * L
Reduction factor = H / G
reduction_formula
Llama 3 8B: H=32, G=8  =>  4x smaller KV cache
llama_example
Tradeoff: queries in same group share K,V projection => less diverse attention patterns

Draw two horizontal bars side by side: left bar full-width labeled 'MHA expressiveness', right bar 3/4-width labeled 'GQA expressiveness (G<H)'. Below both bars write 'quality gap small when trained from scratch'
GQA: bandwidth savings H/G, minimal quality loss => default for large-scale inference
conclusion
